projects.title: Variables and Data Types
projects.description: Declare variables using var, let, const; demonstrate number, string, boolean, null, undefined; show typeof and explicit conversion.
projects.exampleText: let name = 'Alice'; let age = 25; let isStudent = true; console.log(typeof age);
projects.learningOutcome: Understanding variable declaration and primitive types.
projects.logicExplanation: Variables store data values. JavaScript has `var`, `let`, and `const`. Primitive data types include numbers, strings, booleans, null, undefined, symbol, and bigint. Type conversion can be explicit (using `Number()`, `String()`) or implicit (coercion).
projects.codeExplanation: The code demonstrates declaring variables with different types, performing type checks with `typeof`, and showing type conversion.

projects.title: Arithmetic and Assignment Operators
projects.description: Perform basic arithmetic operations, use compound assignment, and calculate area and compound interest.
projects.exampleText: let a = 10, b = 3; console.log(a % b); a += 5;
projects.learningOutcome: Using arithmetic and assignment operators.
projects.logicExplanation: Arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /, %, **) are used to perform mathematical operations. The assignment operator (=) stores values. Compound assignments (+=, -=, etc.) combine operation and assignment.
projects.codeExplanation: The script calculates the area of a rectangle and compound interest, demonstrating arithmetic and assignment operators.

projects.title: Comparison and Logical Operators
projects.description: Compare values (== vs ===), use logical operators to combine conditions, and demonstrate truthy/falsy.
projects.exampleText: console.log(5 == '5'); console.log(5 === '5'); console.log(!false);
projects.learningOutcome: Difference between == and ===; logical operators.
projects.logicExplanation: This project applies the listed JavaScript concepts to solve a practical problem.
projects.codeExplanation: The code demonstrates the use of the relevant features in a clear, commented way.

projects.title: Template Literals and String Concatenation
projects.description: Build strings using + and template literals (`...`). Create dynamic messages.
projects.exampleText: let name = 'John'; console.log('Hello, ' + name); console.log(`Hello, ${name}`);
projects.learningOutcome: String concatenation and template literals.
projects.logicExplanation: This project applies the listed JavaScript concepts to solve a practical problem.
projects.codeExplanation: The code demonstrates the use of the relevant features in a clear, commented way.

projects.title: Smart Cart Calculator (Type Coercion & Conversion)
projects.description: Simulate a shopping cart where prices are stored as strings. Demonstrate implicit and explicit type coercion while calculating totals, discounts, and final payable amount.
projects.exampleText: let price1 = '100'; let price2 = 50; console.log(price1 + price2);
projects.learningOutcome: Understand how JavaScript performs implicit type coercion and how to safely use explicit conversion for calculations.
projects.logicExplanation: The program simulates a cart with mixed data types (string and number prices). It shows how JavaScript implicitly converts types during operations like addition and subtraction. It then uses explicit conversion (Number, String) to correctly calculate totals, apply discounts, and format output. It also demonstrates edge cases like invalid numeric conversion.
projects.codeExplanation: The code defines cart prices as both strings and numbers, performs operations showing implicit coercion, then fixes them using explicit conversion. It calculates total cost, applies discount logic, and prints formatted results using template literals.

projects.title: Advanced Student Grading System (If-Else + Nested Logic)
projects.description: Build a grading system that calculates grade, pass/fail status, division, and remarks using if-else, nested conditions, and logical operators.
projects.exampleText: let score = 82; let attendance = 75;
projects.exampleOutput: Grade: B | Division: First | Status: Pass | Remark: Good
projects.learningOutcome: Use multi-branch conditions, nested if statements, logical operators, and real-world decision making.
projects.logicExplanation: The program evaluates a student's score and attendance. It assigns grades using if-else-if, determines pass/fail using nested conditions, calculates division based on score ranges, and applies extra rules like attendance criteria. It also demonstrates short-circuit evaluation using logical operators.
projects.codeExplanation: The code defines student score and attendance, applies grading logic using if-else-if, uses nested if to check pass conditions, assigns division and remarks, and prints a formatted result using template literals.

projects.title: Ternary Operator – Even or Odd
projects.description: Use the ternary operator to check if a number is even or odd.
projects.exampleText: let result = (num % 2 === 0) ? 'Even' : 'Odd';
projects.learningOutcome: Concise conditional expression.
projects.logicExplanation: The ternary operator (`condition ? expr1 : expr2`) is a concise way to write simple if‑else statements. It returns one of two expressions based on the condition.
projects.codeExplanation: The code checks if a number is even or odd using a ternary operator and prints the result.

projects.title: Switch – Day of Week
projects.description: Given a number (1‑7), use switch to output the day name.
projects.exampleText: switch(day) { case 1: return 'Monday'; ... default: return 'Invalid'; }
projects.learningOutcome: Using switch with break.
projects.logicExplanation: The `switch` statement evaluates an expression and executes the matching case. `break` prevents fall‑through. It's useful for multiple discrete values.
projects.codeExplanation: A day‑of‑week converter uses `switch` to return the day name from a number.

projects.title: For Loop – Sum and Multiplication Tables
projects.description: Calculate sum of 1..100, and print a multiplication table (e.g., 5x1 to 5x10).
projects.exampleText: for(let i=1;i<=100;i++) sum+=i; for(let i=1;i<=10;i++) console.log(`5 x ${i} = ${5*i}`);
projects.learningOutcome: Basic for loop usage.
projects.logicExplanation: The `for` loop repeats a block a specific number of times, using an initializer, condition, and increment. It is ideal for iterating over ranges and arrays.
projects.codeExplanation: The script prints multiplication tables and calculates the sum of numbers from 1 to 100 using a for loop.

projects.title: While Loop – Password Guesser
projects.description: Simulate a simple password guesser using a while loop that breaks when correct.
projects.exampleText: let pass = 'secret'; let guess = ''; while(guess !== pass) { guess = prompt('Enter password:'); }
projects.learningOutcome: While loop with break.
projects.logicExplanation: The `while` loop repeats as long as a condition remains true. It's suitable when the number of iterations is unknown.
projects.codeExplanation: A simple password guesser uses a `while` loop to prompt until the correct password is entered.

projects.title: Do‑While – Menu

projects.exampleText: do { choice = prompt('1. Add 2. Exit'); } while(choice !== '2');
projects.learningOutcome: Do‑while guarantees one execution.
projects.logicExplanation: The `while` loop repeats as long as a condition remains true. It's suitable when the number of iterations is unknown.
projects.codeExplanation: A simple password guesser uses a `while` loop to prompt until the correct password is entered.
